message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>NHS England guidance was issued in November 2020 for use of COVID Oximetry @home (informally named CO@h) to detect early deterioration of patients with COVID-19 in primary and community care settings.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    With the approval of NHS England we conducted a retrospective cohort study between 4
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    October 2020 and 10
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    July 2021, using the primary care records for 57 million people registered at an English general practice. We identified patients with pulse oximetry coding (either specific CO@H codes or non-specific COPD005 codes) and described their characteristics.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We identified 18,473 individuals with a CO@h code, and 1,581,665 with a non-specific COPD005 code related to pulse oximetry. Recording of CO@h codes varied according to patient demographics, region and practice software system (58.3 per 100,000 in TPP vs 13.2 in EMIS).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Our study shows that whilst CO@h codes were used in GP records, use of less specific COPD005 codes instead of new SNOMED CT codes for CO@h may have persisted.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Uptake of service specific codes for the COVID oximetry @Home Pulse Oximetry service: an analysis of 57 million patients’ primary care records using OpenSAFELY
